food_subgroup: Pizza — topped flatbread with cured meat, cheese, and vegetables

serving.natural_language_portion: One whole pizza of approximately 350 g, as typically served at an Italian restaurant, comprising a thin or medium crust base topped with smoked duck ham, caramelized onions, mushrooms, and mozzarella cheese.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pizza with meat topping, NS as to type of crust (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite); supplemented with USDA data for duck meat and mozzarella cheese
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for duck prosciutto pizza. Values are estimated by compositing USDA SR Legacy data for: (1) pizza with meat topping (FDC ID 172004, per 100 g), (2) smoked duck/cured duck meat (FDC ID 05140 analogue), and (3) mozzarella cheese (FDC ID 170845). The composite reflects the described toppings: smoked duck ham, caramelized onions, mushrooms, and mozzarella on a standard pizza base. Energy and macros are weighted estimates. This is an Italian restaurant dish not present in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN; USDA is the highest applicable approved source.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283. Pizza, cheese, thin crust (GI ~50, glucose reference).
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for duck prosciutto pizza. The closest analogous food in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International Tables is cheese pizza on a thin/regular crust (GI ~50, glucose reference). Duck prosciutto pizza shares the same wheat-flour base and cheese topping; the additional protein and fat from smoked duck ham and mushrooms would not materially raise the GI above the cheese-pizza reference. GI of 50 is used as a conservative analogous estimate. GL per serving = (50 × 24.5 × 3.5) / 100 = 42.9 (serving size 350 g, carbohydrate 24.5 g per 100 g → 85.75 g per serving; GL = GI × carb_per_serving / 100 = 50 × 85.75 / 100 = 42.9).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~580 mg per 100 g; ~2030 mg per whole pizza serving) from cured duck ham, cheese, and pizza base
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its very high sodium load from smoked duck prosciutto and mozzarella; a single serving can exceed the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~50) but high glycemic load per whole-pizza serving (~43 GL units) due to large refined-wheat carbohydrate base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this pizza in small portions; while the GI is moderate-low, the high carbohydrate load from the wheat-flour crust in a full serving will produce a significant postprandial glucose response and should be accounted for in meal planning.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.6 g per 100 g) from mozzarella and duck fat, combined with high sodium from cured meat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit intake of this dish due to its combined saturated fat and sodium burden; the smoked duck ham contributes both saturated fat and sodium, which together may adversely affect blood pressure and LDL cholesterol levels.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prosciutto d'Anatra is an Italian-style pizza topped with house-smoked duck ham (prosciutto d'anatra), caramelized onions, sautéed mushrooms, and melted mozzarella cheese, baked on a wheat-flour pizza base. The name translates literally as 'duck prosciutto' in Italian, referring to the cured or smoked duck breast that replaces the more common pork prosciutto. The dish is served at Ricciotti, an Italian restaurant in Singapore, where it is positioned as a premium pizza option. The flavour profile is rich and savoury: the smoky, slightly gamey duck ham contrasts with the sweetness of caramelized onions, the earthiness of mushrooms, and the creamy, milky melt of mozzarella. The crust provides a chewy, lightly charred base. Nutritionally, this pizza is energy-dense at approximately 242 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein from duck meat and cheese (~12.5 g per 100 g), moderate fat (~10.8 g per 100 g including ~4.6 g saturated fat), and a significant carbohydrate contribution from the wheat base (~24.5 g per 100 g). Sodium is notably high (~580 mg per 100 g) due to the cured duck ham and cheese, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 50 (low-moderate), analogous to cheese pizza on a standard crust, but the glycemic load per whole serving is high due to the large carbohydrate mass. This dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding gluten or dairy. It contains allergens including gluten and dairy.

Italian pizza with smoked duck Singapore

confidence_reason: No exact match found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central for duck prosciutto pizza specifically. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing USDA SR Legacy data for meat-topped pizza, smoked duck/cured duck meat, and mozzarella cheese. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (cheese pizza, GI ~50) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is transparent, the analogous sources are approved, and the composite is internally consistent, but direct measurement data for this specific dish is not available.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input value 'Grains and staples' to 'Mixed' to accurately reflect composite pizza dish. 2. Nutrient values are estimated composites from USDA FoodData Central approved sources; no direct measurement exists for this specific restaurant dish. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the USDA composite sources used for this estimation. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the USDA composite sources used for this estimation. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the USDA composite sources used for this estimation. 6. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate based on cheese pizza (thin/regular crust) from the University of Sydney GI Database / 2008 International Tables; no direct GI measurement exists for duck prosciutto pizza. 7. Halal status is false because duck prosciutto (cured/smoked duck) from an Italian restaurant context cannot be assumed halal without certification. 8. The cuisine_tags in the input included 'Chicken Dish' which is incorrect for this duck-based pizza; this tag has been disregarded. 9. Price data (SGD 30.52) is preserved from the field survey source record for reference but is not used as a nutritional evidence source.
